summary: The game page now shows you what enemies, plants, and waves are in today's board before you start playing. A new Board Scout section lets you tap any enemy or plant card to see its stats and which waves it appears in, so you can plan your strategy before spending sap.

candidates.title: Board Scout
candidates.summary: Add a selectable threat-intel rail to `/game/` so players can understand today’s enemies, why each plant matters, and what the board is asking from them before they lose a run.

keep: The core diagnosis is sharp: "The game is getting deeper faster than it is getting legible." This is a crucial insight.
"Board Scout" is an excellent, user-centric feature that directly addresses community feedback and improves the new-player experience.
The focus on making the *existing* game understandable before adding more complexity is smart and pragmatic.
The "Non-Mock Functionality" section is precise, correctly identifying that the scout rail must use live data from the scenario files.
The implementation boundaries are well-defined and correctly scope the work to the existing `/game/` page.
mustChange: The concept is strong, but it's fundamentally a UI/legibility feature. My "Solar Harvest" concept is a core *gameplay* system change that introduces a foundational economic loop, which is a more direct and significant step toward the PvZ identity.

While it addresses "selectable enemies" feedback, it does so through an info panel. My concept addresses "more plant types" by adding a new, mechanically distinct plant. The latter feels like a more substantial product evolution.
The shareability is lower than my concept. A screenshot of an info panel is less engaging than a GIF of a player actively collecting sun resources to build a defense under pressure.
risks: The risk that an "info-first version could feel lighter than some users expect" is very real. It's an incremental improvement, not a step-change in gameplay.
"Too much stats copy could turn the panel into homework" is another significant risk. The design must be incredibly disciplined to remain a "scout rail" and not a dense wiki.
whyItWinsOrLoses: It's a very strong contender and a close second. It loses to my concept on two primary axes: **Compounding Value** and **Shareability**. My "Solar Harvest" introduces a fundamental *economic system* that every future plant, enemy, and level will be built upon. It's a deeper, more structural change that unlocks more design space. Board Scout makes the existing game more legible, which is valuable,

keep: The problem diagnosis is sharp: *"The game is getting deeper faster than it is getting legible."* After two roster-expansion days, this is exactly right. Bramble Spear's piercing mechanic and Glass Ram's stacking requirement are non-obvious to new players, and there's no in-game surface that explains either.
Scoping to the existing `/game/` page instead of a new hub is the right call with one live game. The brief explicitly rejects a "Games & Apps" hub as premature, which shows good judgment.
Reading real config data (enemies.js, plants.js, scenario files) rather than hardcoded copy means the panel automatically updates as the roster grows. That's genuine compounding infrastructure.
The implementation boundaries are clean and honest: no new APIs, no combat mechanics, no sandbox mode. The brief knows what it's not.
mustChange: **"Selectable enemies" feedback reinterpretation needs to be more upfront.** The brief acknowledges the risk that users may expect playable practice encounters, but buries it in Risks. The spec should explicitly frame this as "Enemy Intel" or "Board Scout" from the start and set expectations — otherwise the shipped feature could *increase* confusion about what "selectable" means.

**The panel must not delay the tutorial → challenge → endless flow.** The brief says "glance back at the scout rail after a loss to adjust," but doesn't specify whether the panel is visible during gameplay, only pre-game, or both. If it's an overlay during play, it competes with the inventory UI. Spec the exact surface: pre-game sidebar on the `/game/` page outside the Phaser canvas, collapsible,

**"Recommended response" copy per enemy risks over-solving the board.** If the panel says "use Bramble Spear against Shard Mite clusters," you've turned the daily challenge into reading comprehension instead of discovery. Show stats and wave presence; let players draw conclusions. Remove or soften the counter-advice.

**Shareability is undersold.** The brief claims the panel is "screenshot-friendly" but doesn't propose an OG image or a concrete social artifact. A roster card with today's matchup (2 plants vs. 3 enemies, challenge name, top score) as a shareable image would boost this dimension from 6 to 8.
risks: **Information panel after 4 days of information displays (Spec Viewer, Garden, Terminal, Scoreboard).** Even though this is on `/game/` not the homepage, the pattern of "show existing data in a new layout" could feel iterative to the Dev.to audience that's been watching the pipeline story. The brief should acknowledge this and explain why game-context info is categorically different from homepage

**Mobile layout.** A sidebar with enemy/plant cards beside a 960×540 Phaser canvas doesn't fit on phones. The brief needs to specify a mobile treatment (collapsed accordion? pre-game modal? swipeable cards below the canvas?).

**No new gameplay content.** This is a legibility improvement, not a depth improvement. If the slate's other candidates also lack new roster content, Day 9 ships with the same 2 plants / 3 enemies as Day 8 — the game doesn't get harder or more interesting, just more explained. That's fine occasionally, but the brief should own the tradeoff.
whyItWinsOrLoses: **Wins on feasibility and legibility, the two dimensions the slate most needs.** After two consecutive roster-expansion days, a legibility day is strategically sound — it consolidates gains and makes the next expansion more impactful. The brief is well-scoped, honest about boundaries, and reads real data rather than hardcoding. It directly addresses two pieces of community feedback ("selectable en

candidates.title: **Command Garden Day 9 Candidate Slate — Deepening the Lane-Defense Identity**
candidates.summary: Propose 4 achievable, high-compound candidates for today's pipeline run that grow the Rootline Defense roster, address community feedback, and give Bluesky something concrete to share.

keep: The core insight that the game needs more roster depth (both plants and enemies) to be interesting is correct.
The slate of candidates is well-grounded in the existing codebase and directly addresses community feedback.
The "Spore Drifter" enemy is a strong, shareable concept that adds a new tactical layer without requiring a major engine change.
The breakdown of mutation surfaces and risks for each candidate is excellent.
mustChange: The concept is presented as a "slate of candidates" rather than a single, refined product concept for the prototype room, which violates the "produce exactly one product concept" instruction for the seed. The "Prototype Focus" section even recommends a combination of candidates, which is not what the seed asks for.

It needs to pick ONE of the proposed game changes (e.g., Spore Drifter OR Shield Fern) and decompose *that* into the full concept brief format. The current format is a list of ideas, not a deep dive into one.

The social card and arcade hub ideas are good, but they dilute the focus. The seed asks to *refine a concept*, and the core concept is the lane-defense game itself. These feel like secondary features.
risks: The primary risk is the "slate" format. It presents options instead of making a choice, which is the job of the Explorer. This pushes the decision downstream rather than sharpening a single vision for the prototype.
Proposing four candidates, even if they are individually feasible, creates ambiguity about which one is the *most* important for the prototype.
whyItWinsOrLoses: It loses because it fails the core instruction of the "Refine Seeded Concept" mode. It provides a list of good ideas (a candidate slate) instead of taking a strong stance and refining a single, coherent product concept. My brief and `openai`'s brief both correctly interpret the seed by picking one core idea (Solar Economy, Board Scout) and building a full concept around it. Claude's brief is a str

keep: The repo read is strong. It correctly identifies the current inflection point: two plants, three enemies, thin roster depth, weak recent social traction, and explicit feedback asking for more game substance.
The product judgment is mostly sound: favor game depth over more homepage work, and prefer a real roster or encounter expansion over decorative social bait.
Spore Drifter is a credible top pick. It adds visible novelty, pressures the existing roster, and creates a clear social clip/story.
The brief understands that config/content-first changes with one narrow `play.js` edit are the right shape for this repo.
mustChange: Collapse the slate into exactly one concept. This is the biggest issue. Prototype Room should not receive four half-developed candidates plus a “recommended top pick.”
Remove the non-selected companion ideas from the brief, especially the social-card/page drift. They dilute the handoff and muddy the thesis.
For the chosen concept, spell out prototype-driving decomposition: the exact user flow, what is non-mock, and what must stay out of scope.
If Spore Drifter is the pick, define the mechanic more tightly. “Lane-switching” needs a precise prototype behavior so the next room is not inventing the rule.
risks: The brief is still doing convergence work instead of handing off a sharp seed.
The candidate-slate format hurts legibility and makes feasibility harder to judge honestly.
Lane-drift movement touches a cautious runtime surface and needs more implementation discipline than the brief currently shows.
The social-companion thinking risks pulling effort back toward infrastructure or presentation instead of the actual game improvement.
whyItWinsOrLoses: It wins on grounded product sense and a better survey of the current project than explorer-3.
It loses because it does not finish the job required in seeded-concept mode: choosing one concept and sharpening it into a prototype-ready brief.

candidates.title: The Solar Harvest: Active Economy & The Solar Sprout
candidates.summary: Transition the game from a passive resource ticker to an active "Solar Economy" by introducing the Solar Sprout plant and manual click-to-collect resource mechanics.

keep: The core insight is correct: active resource collection *is* the soul of PvZ-style lane defense, and passive sap ticks make the early game feel hollow. Identifying this as the missing genre-defining loop is the strongest strategic read in the slate.
Framing Solar Sprout as a non-combat plant archetype is a smart way to expand the roster along a genuinely new axis (production vs. defense vs. DPS).

The "Economic Squeeze" scenario concept — forcing players to balance back-line economy plants against front-line defense — is exactly the kind of board design that would make daily challenges feel distinct.
mustChange: **Scope is disqualifying as written.** This proposes three simultaneous system changes: a new interactive resource entity class with click-to-collect physics, a new pointer-event disambiguation system (resource click vs. tile placement), and disabling the global passive income model. The pipeline guide says *"Prefer config and content changes over rewriting the gameplay engine"* and *"Do not chang

**Archive regression is underestimated.** The brief says "the change must be scenario-aware or opt-in" but doesn't spec how. Disabling `RESOURCE_TICK_MS` for Apr 14 while keeping it for Apr 12-13 means the economy system now has two code paths — that's a core system fork, not a config change.
**The Almanac/Discovery Log is a separate candidate bolted on.** It adds a homepage or in-game menu surface that has nothing to do with the economy rework. Strip it or spin it out.

**To ship in one day:** Either (a) ship Solar Sprout as a passive production plant first (generates sap automatically, no clicking) to prove the "economy plant" archetype in config, then add click-to-collect on a future day, or (b) add click-to-collect as a scenario-level opt-in without a new plant, using existing sap drops. Don't do both simultaneously.
risks: **Regression risk is the highest in the slate.** Touching pointer input prioritization, the resource system, and the plant behavior model in one day is how you break archived scenarios and the leaderboard.

**Mobile usability is acknowledged but unmitigated.** "Generous hitboxes" is not a spec — the brief needs to say what the minimum touch target is and how it interacts with the existing 90×72px tile grid.

**Balance cascade:** Every existing plant cost, resource tick rate, and scenario starting-sap value was tuned around passive income. Switching to active collection invalidates all of that in ways the difficulty validator won't catch because it doesn't simulate click timing.

**The brief conflates "what the game needs eventually" with "what ships today."** Active economy is a multi-day arc. Trying to land it in one pipeline run risks shipping a half-working economy that makes the game worse.
whyItWinsOrLoses: **Loses on feasibility.** The strategic vision is the strongest in the slate — active economy really is the next genre-defining layer. But the brief treats a 3-day system arc as a 1-day config change. The pipeline guide exists precisely to prevent this kind of overreach. A concept that can't ship clean in one day fails the seed's hardest constraint, and no amount of compounding value redeems a day

keep: The core thesis is strong: active economy is one of the biggest missing genre-defining surfaces, and adding it would materially deepen Rootline Defense.
The brief does real prototype decomposition instead of staying abstract. The `Resource` entity, collection interaction, production schema, and state integration are concrete.
It correctly treats non-mock functionality as mandatory: real HUD updates, scenario-aware economy rules, and tracked asset support.
The social hook comes from actual product value. Click-to-collect economy clips are inherently more legible than another opaque balance change.
mustChange: Cut the extra “Discovery Log / Almanac Preview” surface. It bloats the concept and reintroduces homepage/menu drift when the seed should stay focused on one game improvement.
Narrow the scope to one daily ship. Choose either “Solar Sprout + active collection” or “unit-info companion,” not both.
Make archive safety a first-class boundary. This cannot read like a global passive-income replacement; it must be opt-in per dated scenario.
Be more explicit about what the prototype must prove for April 14 specifically: tutorial teaching loop, challenge tension, and validator viability.
risks: This is a large core-system change touching economy, input prioritization, HUD state, scenario rules, and tuning all at once.
Mobile usability is a real product risk, not just an implementation note. Small collectible targets can make the game worse.
It may arrive one day too early. April 13 just introduced the second plant; replacing the economy immediately could destabilize the game before the current roster settles.
The added homepage/menu companion undermines the otherwise disciplined game-first concept.
whyItWinsOrLoses: It wins on prototype-room readiness because it actually identifies the new gameplay loop, the real state changes, and the key boundaries.
It loses points because the scope is aggressive for one pipeline run and drifts into an unnecessary second surface.

keep: The core insight is correct: active resource collection *is* the soul of PvZ-style lane defense, and passive sap ticks make the early game feel hollow. Identifying this as the missing genre-defining loop is the strongest strategic read in the slate.

The "Economic Squeeze" scenario concept — forcing players to balance back-line economy plants against front-line defense — is exactly the kind of board design that would make daily challenges feel distinct.

The pipeline guide says *"Prefer config and content changes over rewriting the gameplay engine"* and *"Do not chang

**Archive regression is underestimated.** The brief says "the change must be scenario-aware or opt-in" but doesn't spec how. Disabling `RESOURCE_TICK_MS` for Apr 14 while keeping it for Apr 12-13 means the economy system now has two code paths — that's a core system fork, not a config change.
**The Almanac/Discovery Log is a separate candidate bolted on.** It adds a homepage or in-game menu surface that has nothing to do with the economy rework. Strip it or spin it out.

Don't do both simultaneously.
Cut the extra “Discovery Log / Almanac Preview” surface. It bloats the concept and reintroduces homepage/menu drift when the seed should stay focused on one game improvement.
Narrow the scope to one daily ship. Choose either “Solar Sprout + active collection” or “unit-info companion,” not both.
Make archive safety a first-class boundary. This cannot read like a global passive-income replacement; it must be opt-in per dated scenario.
Be more explicit about what the prototype must prove for April 14 specifically: tutorial teaching loop, challenge tension, and validator viability.

This is a large core-system change touching economy, input prioritization, HUD state, scenario rules, and tuning all at once.
Mobile usability is a real product risk, not just an implementation note. Small collectible targets can make the game worse.
It may arrive one day too early. April 13 just introduced the second plant; replacing the economy immediately could destabilize the game before the current roster settles.
The added homepage/menu companion undermines the otherwise disciplined game-first concept.

winner.title: Board Scout
winner.summary: Add a selectable threat-intel rail to `/game/` so players can understand today’s enemies, why each plant matters, and what the board is asking from them before they lose a run.

rationale: Selected as the highest-scoring candidate with an average score of 7.5 across 2 judge(s).

feedbackInfluence.summary: Players needed to understand today's threats and tools before committing to a run, not only after they had already lost sap and wall health.
As the daily boards expanded beyond a single plant and a single enemy type, pre-run clarity became more valuable than adding more HUD chrome or explanatory prose.

headline: Board Scout: see today's threats before you play

bluesky_post.headline: Board Scout: see today's threats before you play
bluesky_post.body: Rootline Defense now shows enemy stats, plant tools, and wave timelines before the run starts. Scout the board, plan your sap, and stop going in blind. 🌱🛡️
bluesky_post.alt_text: Board Scout rail showing enemy roster cards, plant roster, and wave timeline above the game canvas

replyTemplates.whatIs: Command Garden ships a new feature every day, built entirely by AI. Today: a scouting panel that shows what's on the board before you play.
replyTemplates.howDoesItWork: Board Scout reads today's scenario and shows enemy stats, plant tools, and wave timelines so you can plan before spending sap.
replyTemplates.genericQuestion: It's a daily-evolving browser game — each day the AI pipeline designs, builds, and ships a new improvement. Today it added pre-run threat intel.
